blocks.content: She was in her room by nine.

The house had gone quiet — her parents to their end of it, Maya presumably in her room with her books and her particular brand of late-evening restlessness. Cassandra sat on her bed and didn't turn on the overhead light, just the small one on the nightstand that made everything look softer than it was.

She wasn't ready to sleep. She was in that particular space — the one where you've made a decision and the decision has settled and there's nothing left to do except wait for tomorrow to arrive, and waiting requires occupying the space until then.
She picked up her phone. Opened music.

Not for anything specific — just the habitual reaching for sound that people do when the quiet is the kind that has too much in it. She scrolled without looking for anything. Old playlists. A few albums she hadn't touched in months. Recommendations from a Friday afternoon in February that she barely remembered making.
She stopped on a song she'd heard once before, somewhere she couldn't place — a party, maybe, or something in the background of a drive. She pressed play without any particular expectation.
The first few notes came in.

She sat with it. Let it run. Her mind had been on Ethan all evening — the decision, the park, the wall, the way the fountain light moved — and the song had a quality that felt true to that. Not sentimental. Not obvious. Something quieter: the kind of song that understands that the biggest things live in small moments.
She played it again from the beginning.
